findings.title: MCP record_decision writes to hardcoded path without config
findings.description: The `record_decision` tool hardcodes `.noxaudit/decisions.jsonl` as the decisions path rather than reading from a loaded config. This bypasses any custom `decisions.path` configured in `noxaudit.yml` and may write to an unexpected location.
findings.suggestion: Load config in the tool or accept the decisions path as a parameter, similar to how `run_audit` loads config.

findings.title: MCP run_audit tool executes arbitrary audit without authentication
findings.description: The `run_audit` MCP tool runs a full codebase audit (consuming API credits) when called by any MCP client. There is no authentication or rate-limiting on the MCP server, meaning any tool connected to the stdio transport can trigger costly API calls.
findings.suggestion: Document this risk clearly and consider adding a confirmation step or budget check before executing audits via MCP.

findings.title: Telegram bot token logged indirectly via URL construction
findings.description: The bot token is embedded directly in the URL string: `f'https://api.telegram.org/bot{token}/sendMessage'`. If any HTTP debug logging or proxy intercepts the request, the full URL including the token will be logged.
findings.suggestion: Pass the token as a header or path parameter that is masked in logs, or at minimum document that HTTP debug logging should be disabled in production.

findings.title: Shell command injection risk via subprocess with user-controlled strings
findings.description: The `_create_issue` function constructs a `gh issue create` command where `title`, `body`, `labels`, and `assignees` come from AI-generated findings. While `subprocess.run` with a list (not `shell=True`) is used, extremely long or specially crafted strings from AI output could cause unexpected behavior. More critically, the `labels` list built as `list(config.labels) + [f'noxaudit:{severity}']` passes user-configurable label values directly.
findings.suggestion: Validate and sanitize all values passed to subprocess arguments; truncate title/body lengths; restrict labels to alphanumeric characters.

findings.title: JSON parsing of AI response without error handling
findings.description: In `_parse_response` and `_build_user_message`, `json.loads(text.strip())` is called without a try/except. If the AI returns malformed JSON (or an error message), this raises an unhandled `json.JSONDecodeError` that propagates up and crashes the audit run.
findings.suggestion: Wrap `json.loads` in try/except JSONDecodeError and return an empty findings list with a warning log when parsing fails.

findings.title: JSON parsing of AI response without error handling
findings.description: Both `_parse_text` and `_parse_response` call `json.loads(text.strip())` without a try/except. A malformed or unexpected response from the Gemini API will raise an unhandled `json.JSONDecodeError`.
findings.suggestion: Wrap `json.loads` in try/except JSONDecodeError and return an empty findings list with a warning.

findings.title: JSON parsing of AI response without error handling
findings.description: `_parse_text` calls `json.loads(text.strip())` without error handling. An unexpected response from the OpenAI API will crash the audit.
findings.suggestion: Wrap `json.loads` in try/except JSONDecodeError.

findings.title: Path traversal risk: repo path from config expanded without validation
findings.description: `os.path.expanduser(r['path'])` is applied to the repo path from YAML config, which is then passed to file-gathering functions that recursively glob the filesystem. A misconfigured or malicious config pointing to `/` or `~` could cause the tool to read and transmit the entire filesystem to an AI provider.
findings.suggestion: Validate that the resolved repo path is within expected bounds (e.g., within the working directory or an allowlist), and warn if the path is unusually broad.

findings.title: Action installs noxaudit directly from git main branch
findings.description: The action step `pip install git+https://github.com/atriumn/noxaudit.git` installs the latest commit from the main branch at runtime. This means any push to main (including a supply-chain compromise) immediately affects all users of the action without a version pin.
findings.suggestion: Pin to a specific release tag or commit SHA: `pip install git+https://github.com/atriumn/noxaudit.git@v0.1.0` or publish to PyPI and use `pip install noxaudit==0.1.0`.

findings.title: GitHub token passed as GH_TOKEN but action docs reference GITHUB_TOKEN secret
findings.description: The action sets `GH_TOKEN: ${{ inputs.github-token }}` in the environment, but the `noxaudit/issues.py` code uses the `gh` CLI which reads `GITHUB_TOKEN` by default, not `GH_TOKEN`. The two names may behave differently depending on the `gh` CLI version. The documentation in `github-issues.md` says to set `GITHUB_TOKEN` but the action uses `GH_TOKEN`.
findings.suggestion: Use `GITHUB_TOKEN` as the environment variable name to match `gh` CLI conventions, or verify that `gh` CLI also accepts `GH_TOKEN` on the target runner.

findings.title: MCP get_findings_for_diff runs git subprocess without timeout error handling
findings.description: The `get_findings_for_diff` tool wraps `subprocess.run` in a try/except for `TimeoutExpired` and `FileNotFoundError`, but the timeout is only 10 seconds. In a large repo or slow filesystem, this could silently return an error message instead of results.
findings.suggestion: The current handling is reasonable; consider increasing the timeout or making it configurable.

findings.title: Module docstring references old project name 'Nightwatch'
findings.description: The package docstring reads `"""Nightwatch: Nightly AI-powered codebase audits."""` but the project was renamed to Noxaudit.
findings.suggestion: Update the docstring to `"""Noxaudit: Nightly AI-powered codebase audits."""`

findings.title: Report title still uses 'Nightwatch' brand name
findings.description: In `generate_report`, the report title is `f"# Nightwatch Report: {_focus_display(result.focus)}"`. The project has been renamed to Noxaudit, so this produces incorrectly branded reports.
findings.suggestion: Change the title to `f"# Noxaudit Report: {_focus_display(result.focus)}"`

findings.title: README focus areas table is missing 'testing' focus area
findings.description: The README's Focus Areas table lists only 6 areas (security, docs, patterns, performance, hygiene, dependencies) but the code and docs elsewhere list 7 areas including 'testing'. The `FOCUS_AREAS` dict in `noxaudit/focus/__init__.py` confirms 7 areas.
findings.suggestion: Add a 'testing' row to the Focus Areas table in README.md.

findings.title: README Supported Models table lists non-existent models
findings.description: The README lists models like `gpt-5.2`, `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5-nano`, `gemini-3-flash` which appear to be aspirational/invented model names. These are presented as current reality with pricing, which would mislead users trying to configure these models.
findings.suggestion: Use only models that are publicly available and verifiable, or clearly mark the table as an example/estimate.

findings.title: CHANGELOG release date is in the future
findings.description: The `[0.1.0]` release is dated `2026-02-15`, which is a future date (relative to any realistic publication date). This creates confusion about when the package was actually released.
findings.suggestion: Use the actual release date.

findings.title: Configuration guide documents 'notifications' config with wrong schema
findings.description: The configuration guide shows `notifications: - channel: telegram\n  target: "YOUR_CHAT_ID"` but the Telegram notification implementation reads from `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` and `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` environment variables. The `target` field from config is passed as `chat_id` to `send_telegram()`, but there is no documented relationship between the config `target` and the env var `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` — one might override the other.
findings.suggestion: Clarify in the docs whether `target` in config overrides the `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` env var, or if both must be set.

findings.title: Configuration reference shows wrong default for provider_rotation
findings.description: The reference table says `repos[].provider_rotation` default is `[gemini]`, which matches the code. However, the Quick Start guide's example config uses `provider_rotation: [anthropic]` and README examples also use anthropic as the default, creating inconsistency in the docs.
findings.suggestion: Make the default consistent across all docs, or note that the default differs from the recommended quickstart.

findings.title: First audit guide references 'testing' as a focus area in decisions guide but omits it from status output example
findings.description: The status command example output in `first-audit.md` shows focus areas but doesn't list 'testing', while the code has 7 focus areas. Minor inconsistency in documentation examples.
findings.suggestion: Update the example status output to include all 7 focus areas.

findings.title: Duplicate file gathering and provider initialization logic between _submit_repo and _run_repo_sync
findings.description: The functions `_submit_repo` and `_run_repo_sync` have nearly identical code for: file gathering, provider resolution, `_maybe_prepass` call, decisions loading, prompt building, and dry-run handling. This is significant duplication (~50 lines each) that will diverge over time.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `_prepare_audit` helper that returns (files, provider, prompt, decision_context) to eliminate duplication.

findings.title: Duplicate _build_user_message and _format_files across all three providers
findings.description: The methods `_build_user_message` and `_format_files` are copy-pasted identically across `AnthropicProvider`, `GeminiProvider`, and `OpenAIProvider`. Any change to the message format must be applied in three places.
findings.suggestion: Move `_build_user_message` and `_format_files` to `BaseProvider` in `providers/base.py`.

findings.title: Duplicate _make_finding_id implementation across all three providers
findings.description: The `_make_finding_id` method is copy-pasted identically in `AnthropicProvider`, `GeminiProvider`, and `OpenAIProvider`. The test `test_matches_anthropic_id_logic` in `test_openai_provider.py` explicitly tests that they match, indicating this is known duplication.
findings.suggestion: Move `_make_finding_id` to `BaseProvider`.

findings.title: Duplicate JSON parsing logic (_parse_text / _parse_response) across providers
findings.description: The JSON-from-markdown-block extraction and findings parsing logic is copy-pasted across all three providers with minor variations. `GeminiProvider` has both `_parse_text` and `_parse_response` which overlap significantly.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `_parse_findings_json(text, default_focus)` static method in `BaseProvider`.

findings.title: File exclusion logic duplicated between gather_files and gather_files_combined
findings.description: `BaseFocus.gather_files` and the module-level `gather_files_combined` both implement the same exclusion logic (hardcoded excludes + user patterns, size check, permission error handling). This duplication means fixes must be applied twice.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `gather_files` to call `gather_files_combined([self], ...)` or extract shared file-filtering logic into a helper.

findings.title: Inconsistent import style: some imports inside functions, most at module level
findings.description: Several functions in `cli.py` use deferred imports inside the function body (e.g., `from noxaudit.focus import FOCUS_AREAS` inside `estimate`, `from noxaudit.mcp.state import load_latest_findings` inside `_load_findings_for_baseline`). Most other imports are at module level. This inconsistency makes it harder to understand the dependency graph.
findings.suggestion: Move all imports to module level unless there is a specific reason to defer (e.g., optional dependency).

findings.title: PROVIDERS dict populated via conditional import but OpenAI availability not checked consistently
findings.description: In `runner.py`, `OpenAIProvider` is conditionally added to `PROVIDERS` at module load time. But `GeminiProvider` is imported unconditionally at the top and would fail if `google-genai` is not installed. The `providers/__init__.py` wraps the import in try/except, but `runner.py` imports directly from `noxaudit.providers.gemini`.
findings.suggestion: Follow the same conditional import pattern for GeminiProvider in runner.py, or ensure all optional providers are handled consistently.

findings.title: No tests for run_audit with real provider error handling
findings.description: The `run_audit`, `_run_repo_sync`, and `_retrieve_repo` functions have no tests covering error paths: what happens when the provider raises an exception, when files are empty mid-audit, or when `save_report` fails. These are critical paths with no test coverage.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for error paths in runner functions, including provider exceptions and file I/O failures.

findings.title: GitHub issue creation code has zero test coverage
findings.description: The `create_issues_for_findings`, `_issue_exists`, `_create_issue`, `_gh_available`, and `_gh_authenticated` functions in `issues.py` have no tests at all. This is mutation/side-effect code that interacts with external services.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests with mocked `subprocess.run` calls to cover the issue creation flow, deduplication check, and error handling paths.

findings.title: prepass.py has no dedicated unit tests
findings.description: `run_prepass`, `enrich_files`, `build_classification_prompt`, and `_severity_to_tier` in `prepass.py` have no dedicated unit tests. The CLI integration tests cover the pre-pass flow at a high level but don't test tier mapping, content enrichment, or edge cases.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for `_severity_to_tier`, `enrich_files`, and `run_prepass` with mocked provider.

findings.title: build_estimate_report function has no direct unit tests
findings.description: The `build_estimate_report` function in `pricing.py` contains complex formatting and conditional logic (tiered pricing display, prepass section, alternatives) but is only tested indirectly through the CLI. Direct unit tests would catch regressions in the report format.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for `build_estimate_report` covering: standard pricing, tiered pricing display, prepass section, alternatives section.

findings.title: filter_findings file change detection path not fully tested
findings.description: The `filter_findings` function's file-change detection branch (where `file_hash` differs) is tested in `test_baseline.py` but only through the baseline integration path. The direct `filter_findings` unit tests in `test_decisions.py` don't test the file hash mismatch case.
findings.suggestion: Add a test in `test_decisions.py` that directly tests `filter_findings` when the file has changed since the decision was recorded.

findings.title: TestRunDryRun.test_off_focus does not assert meaningful behavior
findings.description: The test `test_off_focus` invokes the run command with `--focus off` and only asserts `exit_code == 0`. It does not verify that no files were audited or that the output is correct (e.g., 'Nothing to audit' message).
findings.suggestion: Assert on the output content to verify that `off` focus results in no audit being attempted.

findings.title: CostLedger.LEDGER_PATH is a class-level mutable Path constant
findings.description: Using a class-level `LEDGER_PATH = Path(".noxaudit/cost-ledger.jsonl")` requires all tests to use `mock.patch.object(CostLedger, 'LEDGER_PATH', ...)`. The ledger path is not configurable via the config system, so it always writes to the current working directory.
findings.suggestion: Accept an optional path parameter in `append_entry` and `read_entries`, or initialize the path from the config object passed to the runner.

findings.title: LATEST_FINDINGS_FILE constant duplicates the path hardcoded in runner.py and mcp/server.py
findings.description: `LATEST_FINDINGS_FILE = ".noxaudit/latest-findings.json"` is defined in `mcp/state.py`, but `mcp/server.py` also passes `".noxaudit/decisions.jsonl"` as a hardcoded string in `record_decision`. These scattered hardcoded paths are fragile.
findings.suggestion: Centralize all `.noxaudit/` path constants in a single module (e.g., `noxaudit/paths.py`) and import from there.

findings.title: PENDING_BATCH_FILE and LAST_RETRIEVED_FILE are module-level constants not exposed via config
findings.description: The paths `.noxaudit/pending-batch.json` and `.noxaudit/last-retrieved.json` are hardcoded module constants. They cannot be configured by users who may want to run multiple concurrent audits or use a different working directory.
findings.suggestion: Consider adding these to the config or at minimum documenting them as fixed paths.

findings.title: Roadmap doc references internal file '03.5-saas-application.md' that is not in the provided codebase
findings.description: The roadmap document references `[SaaS Application](03.5-saas-application.md)` and `[Strategy & Pricing](../STRATEGY.md)` which are not present in the repository. These are broken internal links.
findings.suggestion: Remove broken links or replace with stub pages/anchors.

findings.title: Duplicate dev dependencies defined in both [project.optional-dependencies.dev] and [dependency-groups.dev]
findings.description: The `pyproject.toml` defines dev dependencies in two places: `[project.optional-dependencies]` (pytest>=8.0, ruff>=0.8) and `[dependency-groups.dev]` (pytest>=9.0.2, ruff>=0.15.1) with different version requirements. This can cause confusion about which is authoritative.
findings.suggestion: Remove one of the duplicate definitions. `[dependency-groups]` is the newer PEP 735 standard; consider using only that.

findings.title: anthropic package listed as a required dependency but should be optional
findings.description: `anthropic>=0.40` is listed in the main `dependencies` array, making it a mandatory install even for users who only want to use Gemini or OpenAI. The README says 'Anthropic is built-in' but this forces all users to install the Anthropic SDK.
findings.suggestion: Move `anthropic` to `[project.optional-dependencies]` with a fallback or keep it required but document clearly that it is bundled intentionally.

findings.title: Missing 'google-api-key' input in action.yml vs openai-api-key documented
findings.description: The `action/action.yml` defines `anthropic-api-key` and `telegram-*` inputs and sets them as env vars, but does not define `openai-api-key` or `google-api-key` inputs even though the docs (`github-actions.md`) shows all three in the secrets setup table and the action reference table lists them.
findings.suggestion: Add `openai-api-key` and `google-api-key` inputs to `action.yml` and pass them as `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `GOOGLE_API_KEY` environment variables.

findings.title: estimate_tokens uses rough 4-chars-per-token heuristic that may significantly undercount for non-ASCII content
findings.description: The `estimate_tokens` function divides character count by 4. For code with many non-ASCII characters, unicode strings, or multi-byte sequences, actual token counts can be 2-3x higher. This can cause pre-pass auto-enable to miss the tier threshold check.
findings.suggestion: Consider using `tiktoken` or the provider's tokenizer for more accurate estimates, or document the known inaccuracy and add a safety margin (e.g., divide by 3 instead of 4).

findings.title: load_decisions reads entire file into memory on every call
findings.description: `load_decisions` reads and parses the entire decisions JSONL file every time it is called. In `runner.py`, `load_decisions` is called at least twice per repo audit (once in `_submit_repo`/`_run_repo_sync` and once in `filter_findings`). For large teams with many decisions (thousands of entries), this is an O(n) read on every call.
findings.suggestion: Cache the loaded decisions within a single audit run, or pass the loaded decisions through the call chain to avoid re-reading.

findings.title: get_prompt_from_file reads from disk on every call with no caching
findings.description: Each focus area's `get_prompt` method calls `get_prompt_from_file` which opens and reads a file from disk every time a prompt is needed. During combined audits, prompts are built via `build_combined_prompt` which calls `get_prompt()` on each focus instance. For repeated calls, this is unnecessary I/O.
findings.suggestion: Cache the prompt content at the class level or instance level after first load.
